Biography
To contact by email, replace +at+ with an @ in above email address

Education:

A.B. Chemistry, Princeton University, 1990; Laboratory of Jeanette Carey

PhD. Molecular and Cell Biology, University of California, Berkeley 1995; Laboratory of Nicholas Cozzarelli

Postdoctoral Studies 1996-2000 Biological Sciences, Stanford University; Laboratory of Sharon Long

Professional Experience: 2000-2001 Scientist/Project Manager ? Human Genome Sequencing Project, Candida Albicans Microarray Project; Stanford Genome and Technology Center

Professional Societies: Association for Women in Science (AWIS) - Editor of the Newsletter, San Francisco Chapter (www.sfawis.com)

Current Support: NIH supplementary grant to promote reentry into biomedical and behavioral research careersResearch:

Individual cells, whether they are a single organism or a small part of a larger organism, need to sense their environment and respond to a multitude of signals in order to successfully grow, propagate and/or perform their specific function. Individual cells need to assess the entirety of their environment and make decisions about how to behave based on multiple inputs - for example, a cell may respond to a particular signal one way if cell density is low, in a different way if cell density is high, and in yet a third way if cell density is low but nutrients are scarce. Cells integrate this information through signal transduction pathways that are interconnected at multiple discrete points. I am interested in how cells process multiple inputs and integrate this information in order to respond appropriately to their environment. I wish to understand at the biochemical level the mechanisms of how different signal transduction pathways are interconnected and how multiple signals are integrated. An understanding of how signal transduction pathways interconnect will lead to a better understanding of how cells make decisions and a better ability to predict how cells will respond to specific changes in their environment. This predictive ability will enable a deeper understanding of a multitude of disease processes, a better ability to design therapeutics to treat these diseases, and the ability to use computer simulations of whole cells or tissues to perform valid and relevant experiments in silico, leading to a considerable savings in time and resources.

MAP kinase pathways are highly conserved eukaryotic signal transduction pathways. They are composed of a series of three kinases that sequentially phosphorylate each other - a MAP kinase kinase kinase (MEKK or MAPKKK) phosphorylates a MAP kinase kinase (MEK or MAPKK), which in turn phosphorylates the MAP kinase (MAPK). Some MAPK pathways also include a scaffold protein which binds two or three of the kinases. The yeast pheromone response MAP kinase pathway is composed of Ste5, the scaffold protein; Ste11, the MEKK; Ste7, the MEK; and Fus3, the MAPK. While this MAP kinase pathway is well characterized genetically, many questions remain about how the Ste5 scaffold works: Do the kinases bind at the same time or do they bind sequentially? Is binding of the kinases to the scaffold cooperative? Does binding to the scaffold affect the enzymatic activity of the kinases by increasing local concentration, or does Ste5 also have an allosteric effect? What is the strength of binding of the different kinases to Ste5 and to each other in the presence of Ste5? Does the phosphorylation state of the kinases affect their binding to Ste5? What is the oligomerization state and kinase composition of the most active Ste5-kinase complex? I am addressing these questions by biochemical reconstitution of the Ste5:Ste11-Ste7-Fus3 scaffold-kinase complex and quantification of the enzymatic activity, stoichiometry and binding affinities of complex formation.Publications:
